programs.name: AHCCCS for Elders
programs.tagline: Free long-term care for Arizona seniors who need nursing home level care but want to stay home.

localEntities.name: Local DES/Family Assistance Administration offices statewide

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ in Arizona and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, AHCCCS for Elders covers all long-term care costs — whether they stay home or move to a facility. This isn't regular health insurance. It's Arizona's Medicaid program specifically for seniors who meet nursing home level of care but can live safely at home with support services.

The program covers personal care aides, adult day programs, home-delivered meals, respite care for family caregivers, and assisted living if needed. Your parent keeps their doctor and Medicare benefits. Because AHCCCS coordinates all care through one team, you won't juggle multiple providers or fight insurance denials.

Income limit: $2,982/month for a single person, $5,964/month for couples (2026). Assets must be under $2,000. Your parent must pass a clinical assessment proving they need nursing home level care, even if they plan to stay home.

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona Plus or call (888) 621-6880 — processing takes weeks to months for full assessment.

steps.title: Submit financial application
steps.description: Apply online at Health-e-Arizona Plus portal or call ALTCS at (888) 621-6880. You'll need income, asset, and residency documentation.

steps.title: Complete nursing home level assessment
steps.description: AHCCCS schedules a clinical assessment to determine if your parent needs daily care assistance. This happens after financial review.

steps.title: Wait for eligibility determination
steps.description: AHCCCS reviews both financial and medical criteria. If approved, services start based on assessed needs and available slots.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Weeks to months for complete financial and medical assessment
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible for home and community services due to limited slots; nursing facility placement typically has shorter waits
applicationGuide.tip: Start the application before your parent needs immediate placement — the assessment process takes time.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: 2026 limits for ALTCS (Arizona Long Term Care System)

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts Toward $2,000 Limit
excluded: Primary home (if equity under $713,000 and intent to return)
Burial plots and prepaid funeral expenses
Life insurance with face value up to $1,500

categories.name: Medical
items: Medicare card and other insurance cards
Recent medical records showing care needs

contentSections.text: Arizona will recover costs from your parent's estate after they pass away if they received services after age 55. The state places a lien on the home that must be paid when the property is sold.
If your parent's income is slightly over the limit, they may still qualify but pay a monthly 'share of cost' (like a deductible) toward their care. Don't assume they make too much without applying.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for AHCCCS?
faqs.answer: Yes, if she intends to return home and the home equity is under $713,000. However, Arizona will place a lien on the home to recover costs after she passes away. The lien must be paid when the home is sold.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $2,982 limit?
faqs.answer: He may still qualify but pay a monthly 'share of cost' — essentially a deductible based on his excess income. For example, if his income is $3,200/month, he might pay $218/month toward his care costs while AHCCCS covers the rest.

faqs.question: Does AHCCCS replace Medicare or work alongside it?
faqs.answer: AHCCCS works alongside Medicare. Your parent keeps their Medicare benefits for doctor visits and hospital care. AHCCCS covers the long-term care services Medicare doesn't — like personal care aides, adult day programs, and assisted living.

faqs.question: How long does the nursing home assessment take?
faqs.answer: The clinical assessment happens after financial approval and typically involves a home visit or phone interview with a nurse assessor. This can add weeks to the overall process, which is why starting early is important.

faqs.question: What if there's a waitlist for home care services?
faqs.answer: Nursing facility placement typically has shorter waits than home and community services. If your parent needs immediate care, they may need to enter a facility first while waiting for home care slots to open up.

faqs.question: Will this affect my parent's other benefits like Social Security?
faqs.answer: No, AHCCCS won't affect Social Security, Medicare, or most other benefits. However, if your parent receives SSI, the coordination can be complex — the AHCCCS office will help sort this out during application.

programs.name: Arizona Long Term Care System (ALTCS)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or other basic activities, ALTCS can cover their nursing home, assisted living, or home care costs.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with basic activities like bathing or dressing, Arizona's Long Term Care System (ALTCS) can cover the full cost of nursing home care, assisted living, memory care, or home care services. Because ALTCS is Arizona's Medicaid program for long-term care, it pays for services your parent's Medicare or private insurance won't cover.

Your parent must meet both financial limits (income under $2,901/month and assets under $2,000 for a single person in 2025) and functional requirements (needing help with at least two daily activities). ALTCS covers the services your parent needs based on their individual care plan — whether that's 24/7 nursing facility care or a few hours of home care each week.

The application process takes 60-90 days and requires careful documentation of both your parent's financial situation and care needs. One mistake on the application results in automatic denial, so accuracy is critical.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need assistance with at least two activities of daily living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, or continence) OR require nursing facility level of care
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or proper immigration status

applicationGuide.summary: Call (888) 621-6880 to request application forms — processing takes 60-90 days.

steps.title: Call ALTCS
steps.description: Call (888) 621-6880 to request application form DE-828 and instructions

steps.title: Gather documentation
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, residency, and physician certification of care needs

steps.title: Complete application accurately
steps.description: Fill out Form DE-828 with no errors — mistakes result in automatic denial

steps.title: Submit for review
steps.description: Application goes through financial review (assets/income) then medical review (care needs assessment)

applicationGuide.tip: Have a family member or attorney help with the application — one mistake causes automatic denial.

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of citizenship or immigration status

categories.name: Financial
items: Documentation of all assets and property

categories.name: Medical
items: Physician's certification of need for long-term care
Documentation of limitations with daily activities (bathing, dressing, etc.)

contentSections.text: ALTCS has a 60-month look-back rule. If your parent gave away assets or sold them below market value within 5 years of applying, they face a penalty period of ineligibility.
A dementia or Alzheimer's diagnosis alone does NOT guarantee eligibility. Your parent must demonstrate they need physical help with at least two daily activities.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for ALTCS?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it's her primary Arizona residence and the home equity is under $752,000 (2026 limit). The house is exempt from the asset limits as long as she lives there or intends to return.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $2,901 limit?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify for ALTCS. The income limits are strict — $2,901/month for a single person in 2025. However, limits are updated each January, so check current amounts when applying.

faqs.question: Does my parent need to be in a nursing home already to apply?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent can apply from home, assisted living, or anywhere else. ALTCS will assess their care needs and determine the appropriate level of services.

faqs.question: What happens if we make a mistake on the application?
faqs.answer: Automatic denial. ALTCS requires error-free applications. Consider having a family member, attorney, or Medicaid planning service help complete the paperwork.

faqs.question: How much of my parent's care will ALTCS actually cover?
faqs.answer: ALTCS covers all medically necessary long-term care services based on your parent's individual care plan — this could be full nursing home costs, assisted living fees, or home care hours as needed.

faqs.question: If my parents are married, do both need to qualify?
faqs.answer: No. Only the spouse needing care must meet the medical and age requirements. The community spouse (staying at home) has different asset protections, determined case-by-case.

programs.name: Arizona PACE Programs
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home, PACE covers all medical care and services through one team.

localEntities.name: InnovAge operates PACE/LIFE centers in Arizona (among other states: California, Colorado, Florida, New Mexico, Pennsylvania, Virginia)
Specific PACE organizations and their service areas not detailed in search results

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs nursing home level care but wants to stay in their community, Arizona PACE Programs provide comprehensive medical and social services through one coordinated team. Because PACE covers all care under one program, your parent never pays deductibles or co-pays for any medical care, medications, or services provided by their PACE team.

PACE serves adults who have been certified as needing nursing home level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other personal care). The average PACE participant is 76 years old with multiple complex medical conditions. If your parent has Medicaid, PACE is fully covered. If not, they pay a monthly premium but still receive all services without additional costs.

Critical limitation: PACE is only available in specific service areas where PACE organizations operate — not all Arizona counties have PACE programs available.

summary: Nursing home level of care certification
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by AHCCCS as meeting nursing home level of care (needing daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other personal care tasks)
otherRequirements: Must live in a PACE organization service area
Must be able to live safely in community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional AHCCCS number to start the application and nursing home level of care assessment.

steps.title: Verify PACE availability in your area
steps.description: Confirm a PACE organization operates in your parent's zip code before applying.

steps.title: Call regional AHCCCS number
steps.description: Central Arizona: 800-564-5465, Northern Arizona: 888-788-4408, Southern Arizona: 866-495-6738.

steps.title: Complete nursing home level of care assessment
steps.description: AHCCCS will assess whether your parent meets the clinical criteria for nursing home placement.

steps.title: Complete PACE intake assessment
steps.description: PACE organization staff will assess whether your parent can live safely in the community with PACE services.

steps.title: Submit financial documentation
steps.description: If seeking Medicaid coverage, provide income and asset documentation to determine eligibility.

applicationGuide.tip: Gather medical records showing your parent's care needs before calling — this speeds up the nursing home level of care assessment.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts (If Using Medicaid)
excluded: Burial plots and $1,500 for burial expenses
Life insurance with face value under $2,000

offices.name: Central Arizona
offices.address: Covers Phoenix metro area and surrounding counties

offices.name: Northern Arizona
offices.address: Covers Flagstaff, Prescott, and northern counties

offices.name: Southern Arizona
offices.address: Covers Tucson metro area and southern counties

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing need for daily care assistance
Physical therapy or occupational therapy evaluations

categories.name: Financial (if using Medicaid)
items: Bank statements for all accounts (3 months)

categories.name: Identity
items: Arizona driver's license or state ID

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide. You must verify a PACE organization operates in your parent's specific area before starting the application process.
Your parent cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans while receiving PACE services. They may need to disenroll during Medicare's annual open enrollment period.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your home is completely exempt from PACE asset limits, regardless of value. PACE is designed to help people stay in their community rather than move to a nursing home.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is too high for Medicaid but he needs PACE services?
faqs.answer: He can still enroll in PACE by paying a monthly premium as a private-pay participant. There are no income limits for PACE itself — only for Medicaid funding. He'll still receive all services without deductibles or co-pays.

faqs.question: Does PACE replace Dad's Medicare and current doctors?
faqs.answer: Yes. Once enrolled, your parent must receive all covered services through the PACE organization's team of doctors, nurses, and specialists. They cannot use Medicare to see outside providers for covered services.

faqs.question: How do I know if PACE is available where Mom lives?
faqs.answer: Call your regional AHCCCS number first. They can tell you immediately if a PACE organization serves your parent's zip code. InnovAge operates some Arizona locations, but coverage varies by county.

faqs.question: What's the difference between needing 'nursing home level of care' but being 'safe in the community'?
faqs.answer: Your parent must be sick enough to qualify for nursing home placement (needing daily help with personal care) but stable enough that PACE services can keep them safe at home. PACE staff assess both requirements during enrollment.

faqs.question: Can Mom leave PACE if she doesn't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE enrollment is voluntary and your parent can disenroll at any time. They would return to regular Medicare and Medicaid coverage (if eligible) and choose new healthcare providers.
